title: Integers that sum to the same value

content.problemStatement:
Alice has a **positive** integer NNN. She is wondering how many ordered pairs of **positive** integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) exist such that i+j=Ni + j = Ni+j=N.

Help Alice figure out the answer.

Note that since the pairs are ordered, (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1) are considered different.

Note that 000 is not considered to be a positive integer.


Input Format
The first and only line of input contains a single integer NNN.



Output Format
Print a single integer, the number of ordered pairs of positive integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that i+j=Ni + j = Ni+j=N.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single positive integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of ordered pairs of positive integers (i, j) such that i + j = N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 no positive pair exists, answer 0. For N=2 only (1,1) works, answer 1. For N=3 the pairs are (1,2) and (2,1), answer 2.
For N=5 possible i values: 1..4 → 4 pairs. For N=10 possible i values: 1..9 → 9 pairs.
All i from 1 to 999 give a valid ordered pair, so answer is 999.

solution.approach: For a fixed N, i can take any value from 1 to N‑1 inclusive, and j is uniquely determined as N‑i. Hence there are exactly N‑1 ordered pairs. If N = 1 there is no valid i, so the answer is 0. The algorithm simply computes max(0, N‑1) for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
